1:
"Now in the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar, Pontius Pilate being governor of Judaea, and Herod being tetrarch of Galilee... and Lysanias the tetrarch of Abilene," The witnesses dwell on the careful dating and its theological point.

Luke fixes the time with unusual precision. Henry's tradition notes the date is "taken notice of, which was not by the other evangelists, that the truth of the thing might be confirmed by the exact fixing of the time." Gill identifies Tiberius as "the third of the Caesars," in whose fifteenth year "John began to preach, Christ was baptized, and began to preach also; so that this year may be truly called the acceptable year of the Lord."

The fathers read the naming of rulers as significant. Gregory observes that "because John came to preach Him who was to redeem some from among the Jews, and many among the Gentiles, therefore the time of his preaching is marked out by making mention of the king of the Gentiles and the rulers of the Jews." Origen draws the contrast with the prophets: "in the word of prophecy spoken to the Jews alone, the Jewish kingdom only is mentioned," but "in the Gospel which was to be proclaimed to the whole world, the empire of Tiberius Caesar is mentioned, who seemed the lord of the whole world."

The divided state of Judaea the witnesses read as a sign. Henry's tradition notes the dating "by the government of the heathen... to show that they were a conquered people, and therefore it was time for the Messiah to come": "the lawgiver was now departed from between Judah's feet" (cf. Genesis 49:10), "and therefore now Shiloh must come." Origen agrees: "because the Jews were to be scattered for their crime of treachery, the Jewish kingdom was shut up into parts under several governors." Bede details the Roman manipulation of even the high-priesthood. The witnesses thus find in the bare chronological frame a quiet sermon: the sceptre fallen from Judah, the land parcelled among foreign-backed tetrarchs, the world gathered under one Caesar — all marking the appointed hour for the forerunner's cry and the coming of the universal King.
2:
"Annas and Caiaphas being the high priests, the word of God came unto John the son of Zacharias in the wilderness." The witnesses dwell on the corrupt priesthood and the divine call to John.

The two high priests betray a disordered state. Henry's tradition notes "God had appointed that there should be but one high priest at a time, but here were two," a sign that they were "a corrupt people, and that therefore it was time that the Messiah should come, to reform them." Bede (on v. 1) explains that "the honour of the High Priest's office was never given to merit or high birth, but the whole affairs of the Priesthood were managed by the Roman power" — Annas deposed yet still wielding influence, Caiaphas the acting priest.

The call to John is described in the language of the prophets. "The word of God came unto John," Henry's tradition observes, "the same expression that is used concerning the Old-Testament prophets" (cf. Jeremiah 1:2); "for John was a prophet, yea, more than a prophet, and in him prophecy revived, which had been long suspended." Chrysostom stresses the divine initiative: "the word of God here mentioned was a commandment, for the son of Zacharias came not of himself, but God moved him." Ambrose (on v. 1) draws the deeper note: "the word of the Lord came to John, that the Church should begin not from man, but from the Word."

The wilderness setting the witnesses find fitting. Henry's tradition: "the word of the Lord came to him in the wilderness; for those whom God fits he will find out, wherever they are. As the word of the Lord is not bound in a prison, so it is not lost in a wilderness." Theophylact notes John "was hid in the wilderness, that no suspicion might arise" that his witness to Christ sprang from mere kinship. The witnesses thus see the true authority of John's ministry set over against the hollow official religion of Annas and Caiaphas: while the priesthood is traded by Roman favour, the genuine word of God passes them by and comes to a prophet in the desert.
3:
"And he came into all the country about Jordan, preaching the baptism of repentance for the remission of sins;" The witnesses dwell on John's coming forth, his baptism, and its meaning.

John leaves his solitude for the people. Henry's tradition notes "he quitted his deserts, and came into the inhabited country... He came first into all the country about Jordan," teaching that "those that are best pleased in their retirements must cheerfully exchange them when God calls them into places of concourse." Ambrose (on v. 1) reads the wilderness whence he came as itself a figure of the Church, "for the barren has more children than she who has an husband."

The baptism he preached the witnesses carefully define. The sign, Henry's tradition notes, "was such as was ordinarily used among the Jews, washing with water... but the meaning of it was repentance for the remission of sins." It carried a double force. First, those baptized "were thereby obliged to repent of their sins" — "to change their mind, and change their way, to cast away from them all their transgressions." Second, "they were thereby assured of the pardon of their sins, upon their repentance": "as the baptism he administered bound them not to submit to the power of sin, so it sealed to them a gracious and pleadable discharge from the guilt of sin" (cf. Ezekiel 18:30).

The witnesses thus see John's baptism as the threshold of the new dispensation, neither a mere ceremony nor a saving rite in itself, but a summons and a seal. It summoned the nation to that inward turning which the gospel requires, and it pledged to the penitent the forgiveness God had promised. John's whole errand, the commentators agree, was preparatory: to bring a careless people to repentance, and so to make them ready for the One whose way he came to prepare (cf. Acts 13:24). The baptism of repentance looked beyond itself to the remission that Christ alone would purchase and bestow. Henry's tradition stresses that John bound his hearers "not to such ceremonious observances as were imposed by the tradition of the elders, but to change their mind and change their way" — an inward turning, not a new ritual, that the gospel might find them ready.
4:
"As it is written in the book of the words of Esaias the prophet, saying, The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight." The witnesses dwell on the prophecy fulfilled and John's office as the voice.

John's ministry fulfils Isaiah. Henry's tradition notes the other Evangelists "had referred us to the same text," Isaiah 40:3, "those words of his which were written for the generations to come"; "John is that voice, a clear distinct voice, a loud voice, an articulate one." The commentators observe that Luke, writing for a wider world, quotes the prophecy more fully than Matthew or Mark, carrying it on through the following verses.

That John is called "the voice" the fathers find significant. Ambrose had drawn the distinction (on v. 1) that "the Church should begin not from man, but from the Word" — and John is but the voice that heralds that Word (cf. John 1:23, where John applies the text to himself). The voice goes before to announce; the Word himself follows. So John's whole dignity lies not in himself but in the One he proclaims.

His task is to prepare the way. Henry's tradition explains: "John's business is to make way for the entertainment of the gospel in the hearts of the people, to bring them into such a frame and temper as that Christ might be welcome to them, and they welcome to Christ." The "way" and the "paths" are the hearts of men, which repentance levels and clears for the coming King. The witnesses thus see in the prophet's ancient word the exact charter of the Baptist's mission: a voice crying in the wilderness, not to be heard for its own sake, but to ready a people for their Lord — making straight, by the call to repentance, the road by which Christ would enter and reign. Henry's tradition adds that Isaiah's words were "written for the generations to come", so that the prophet, centuries before, described the very office John would fill; the forerunner's work was no novelty but the long-appointed preparation for the Lord's appearing.
5:
"Every valley shall be filled, and every mountain and hill shall be brought low; and the crooked shall be made straight, and the rough ways shall be made smooth;" The witnesses dwell on Luke's fuller citation and its spiritual meaning.

Luke alone extends the Isaianic prophecy this far (Isaiah 40:4). Henry's tradition notes that "Luke goes further on with the quotation than Matthew and Mark had done, and applies the following words likewise to John's ministry." The commentators read the levelling of the land as a figure of the gospel's work in the soul.

The transformations are moral. "The humble," Henry's tradition explains, "shall by it be enriched with grace: every valley that lies low and moist shall be filled and be exalted." Conversely, "the proud shall by it be humbled; the self-confident that stand upon their own bottom, and the self-conceited that lift up their own top, shall have contempt put upon them: every mountain and hill shall be brought low" — "if they repent, they are brought to the dust; if not, to the lowest hell." So the gospel reverses the world's order, raising the lowly and abasing the high.

The crooked and the rough also yield. "Sinners shall be converted to God," Henry's tradition continues: "the crooked ways and the crooked spirits shall be made straight; for, though none can make that straight which God hath made crooked, yet God by his grace can make that straight which sin hath made crooked." And the difficulties that hindered the way to God are removed: "the rough ways shall be made smooth; the gospel has made the way to heaven plain and easy to be found, smooth and easy to be walked in." The witnesses thus see in the prophet's imagery a portrait of repentance and grace: the high places of pride brought down, the low places of humility filled, the crooked heart set straight, and every obstacle cleared — all to make a level road on which the salvation of God may come, and on which sinners may walk home to him. Henry's tradition adds that "they that love God's law shall have great peace, and nothing shall offend them", for the rough ways made smooth are the discouragements removed from the path of those who set out toward heaven.
6:
"And all flesh shall see the salvation of God." The witnesses dwell on the universal scope of the salvation Isaiah foretold.

This closing clause crowns Luke's extended citation (Isaiah 40:5). Henry's tradition marks it as the climax: "the great salvation shall be more fully discovered than ever, and the discovery of it shall spread further." The words gather up the purpose of all that has gone before — the levelling of the way is so that the salvation of God may be seen by all.

The reach is universal. "All flesh shall see it," Henry's tradition explains — "not the Jews only, but the Gentiles. All shall see it; they shall have it set before them and offered to them, and some of all sorts shall see it, enjoy it, and have the benefit of it." That Luke alone carries the quotation to this point is fitting, the commentators observe, in the Gospel most concerned to show Christ as the Saviour of the whole world (cf. Isaiah 52:10; Titus 2:11). The "salvation of God" is no abstraction but a Person: the Christ whom John came to herald, set forth for all nations to behold.

The witnesses draw the practical word. The seeing of the salvation, Henry's tradition notes, follows the preparing of the way: "when way is made for the gospel into the heart, by the captivation of high thoughts... by the levelling of the soul and the removing of all obstructions that stand in the way of Christ and his grace, then prepare to bid the salvation of God welcome." The whole prophecy, the witnesses conclude, moves to this end: that across the levelled road of repentance, to Jew and Gentile alike, God's saving Christ should come into open view, offered to all flesh and welcomed by every humbled, prepared heart. John's preparatory cry finds its goal not in reformation merely but in the universal showing of the Saviour. Gill notes the salvation here is to be "seen" — set before men's eyes and offered to them — so that the prophecy promises not a hidden but an openly published deliverance, proclaimed in the gospel to every nation under heaven.
7:
"Then said he to the multitude that came forth to be baptized of him, O generation of vipers, who hath warned you to flee from the wrath to come?" The witnesses dwell on the stinging name, its meaning, and the warning it carried.

John spares neither the crowd nor the great. Chrysostom notes "the dweller in the wilderness... bent not beneath the weight of such respect, but rose up against them and reproved them." Luke addresses the multitudes generally, where Matthew (cf. Matthew 3:7) names the Pharisees and Sadducees specifically (Ellicott, Robertson). Robertson notes the imperfect "he used to say" and the repeated crowds; John's metaphors are all "from the wilderness — vipers, fruits, axe."

The name "generation of vipers" the fathers explain. Chrysostom: "the holy Scripture often gives the names of wild beasts to men, according to the passions which excite them... serpents and vipers for their poison and cunning." The viper-brood are parricides: "the fetus... kills the mother, bursting open the womb in revenge of its father's death; the viper progeny therefore are parricides. Such also were the Jews, who killed their spiritual fathers and teachers." Yet, Chrysostom adds, John does not "blame the Patriarchs, or call them vipers"; he cuts at their vain trust in descent.

The warning is of judgment. Gregory glosses "the wrath to come" as "the awarding of final punishment"; Calvin takes it for "the judgment of God," named as still "to come" because "hypocrites, so long as God spares them, despise boldly all his threatenings." Cyril of Jerusalem turns the figure to exhortation: "be not henceforth a viper... put off the slough of thy former sinful life," for John's baptism is "a redemption from the threat of the fire." The witnesses thus see John opening his ministry not with flattery but with alarm — naming the people's poisonous cunning, exposing their false confidence, and driving them, by the terror of coming wrath, toward a repentance that must be more than a momentary fright. Ambrose reads even the coming of these men with hope — "inspired with prudence to seek repentance of their crimes, dreading with wise devotion the terror of the judgment to come" — though they "still forsake not what is hurtful"; the warning was meant to drive that fear inward to a true change.
8:
"Bring forth therefore fruits worthy of repentance, and begin not to say within yourselves, We have Abraham to our father: for I say unto you, That God is able of these stones to raise up children unto Abraham." The witnesses dwell on the fruits required, the false plea forbidden, and the children from stones.

Repentance must bear fruit. Chrysostom: "it is not sufficient for the penitent to leave off his sins, he must also bring forth the fruits of repentance... just as in order to heal, it will not do to pluck out the arrow only, but we must also apply a salve to the wound." Gregory adds they must be "fruits worthy of repentance," proportioned to the guilt: "the fruit of good works ought not to be equal in the man who has sinned less and the man who has sinned more."

The plea of descent is cut off. Chrysostom explains it "avails them nothing to have Abraham for their father, unless they observed the relationship in respect of virtue"; Cyril of Alexandria, that "what profits the nobleness we inherit through the flesh, unless it be supported by kindred feelings in us?" Basil's image: "neither does the speed of its sire make the horse swift" — "a disgraceful thing for a man to be adorned with the honours of another, when he has no virtue of his own" (cf. John 8:39; Romans 4:12). Wesley sums: "trust not in your being members of the visible Church... for God now requires a change of heart."

The "stones" the fathers read as the Gentiles. Gregory of Nyssa: "having foretold the casting away of the Jews, he goes on to allude to the calling of the Gentiles, whom he calls stones." Ambrose: "what else than stones were they who bowed down to stones... it is prophesied that faith shall be poured into the stony hearts of the Gentiles, and through faith... Abraham shall have sons." Lightfoot notes John may play on the Hebrew words for "stones" and "children," deriding the nation's boast. The witnesses thus see John demolishing the last refuge of the impenitent: privilege without holiness saves no one, for God can raise true children of Abraham — believing Gentiles — out of the most unlikely stones.
9:
"And now also the axe is laid unto the root of the trees: every tree therefore which bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire." The witnesses dwell on the imminence of judgment and the doom of the fruitless.

The axe is laid, but not yet fully struck. Cyril of Jerusalem reads it as "the deadly wrath of God which fell upon the Jews on account of the impieties they practised against Christ"; he notes John says not that the axe is "fixed to the root" but "laid... near the root," for "though the branches were cut down, the tree itself was not yet entirely destroyed. For a remnant of Israel shall be saved." Gill names the instrument: "the Romans were now among them, the axe in God's hand," about to bring "utter ruin... upon their nation, city, and temple."

Gregory offers a wider reading: "the tree represents the whole human race... but the axe is our Redeemer, who by the handle and iron is held indeed in the hand of man, but strikes by the power of God." And it is laid "at the root, not the branches": "when the whole family together with the parent is removed, the unfruitful tree is cut off from the very root."

The fruitless tree is for the fire. The standard is not leaves but fruit — and good fruit (cf. Matthew 7:19; Luke 13:7). Chrysostom notes "God created man... fond of employment," so that "as idleness is an evil, so also is an unworthy activity"; the axe now "lies near, not indeed actually cutting, but only striking terror." Ambrose draws the application: "let him then that is able bring forth fruit unto grace, him who ought, unto repentance. The Lord is at hand seeking for his fruit, who shall cherish the fruitful, but rebuke the barren." The witnesses thus see John pressing the urgency home: judgment stands ready at the very root, and only fruit — the evidence of real repentance — will save the tree from the fire. Cyril of Jerusalem warns the hypocrite plainly: "the Judge is inexorable; put away thine hypocrisy."
10:
"And the people asked him, saying, What shall we do then?" The witnesses dwell on the awakened conscience and the practical turn of John's ministry.

The preaching pierced the hearers. Gregory notes "the hearts of his hearers were troubled, and sought for advice from him"; Clarke describes "an uncommon effusion of that Spirit which convinces of sin," so that "the people... earnestly begin to inquire what they must do to be saved" (cf. Acts 2:37; Acts 16:30). Robertson notes the imperfect "repeatedly asked," and that the "then" (oun) "refers to the severe things already said."

Three classes come in turn. Origen: "three classes of men are introduced as inquiring of John... the multitude... the Publicans... and the soldiers." Ellicott marks that these questions "are peculiar to St. Luke," and reflect that "the work of the Baptist was not that of a mere preacher of repentance": "confession of sins followed naturally... that was followed, as naturally, by guidance for the conscience. St. Luke, as a physician of the soul, may well have delighted to place on record this example of true spiritual therapeutics."

The guidance, the witnesses note, is moral and practical. Calvin observes that "a true feeling of repentance produces in the mind of the poor sinner an eager desire to know what is the will or command of God"; and John, rather than prescribing ceremonies, "recommends the duties of charity, and of the second Table of the Law" — "not that God disregards the outward profession of godliness... but that this is a surer mark of distinction, and less frequently leads to mistakes." Hypocrites, he adds, "labour strenuously to prove themselves worshippers of God by the performance of ceremonies — paying no regard, however, to true righteousness." Wesley sums the whole: "Cease to do evil, learn to do well. These are the fruits worthy of repentance." The witnesses thus see the question "What shall we do?" as the first true sign of repentance, and John's answer as its test: a changed life, shown in plain duties, not in religious display. Robertson notes the deliberative aorist — "what are we to do?" — repeated by each class in turn (vv. 12, 14); the question is not idle curiosity but the cry of consciences pressed by the severe words John had just spoken.
11:
"He answereth and saith unto them, He that hath two coats, let him impart to him that hath none; and he that hath meat, let him do likewise." The witnesses dwell on the charity required of the people.

To the multitude John prescribes practical mercy. Theophylact notes that "to the Publicans and soldiers he gives a commandment to abstain from evil, but the multitudes, as not living in an evil condition, he commands to perform some good work." Gregory presses the cost: "because a coat is more necessary for our use than a cloak, it belongs to the bringing forth of fruits worthy of repentance, that we should divide with our neighbours not only our superfluities but those which are absolutely necessary." Robertson notes the word (chitōn) is "the inner and less necessary undergarment."

The duty rests on love of neighbour. Basil: "he is proved to love his neighbour less than himself, who does not share with him in his distress, those things which are even necessary to himself"; "of the works worthy of repentance these are enjoined before all others." Yet Ambrose marks a limit: "in mercy itself a proportion is observed... each individual is not to deprive himself of all, but what he has to share it with the poor."

The witnesses draw out the heart of the matter. Barnes notes "it is remarkable that one of the first demands of religion is to do good," for "the nature of religion is to do good," it "requires self-denial," and it is "to imitate Jesus Christ, who, though he was rich, yet for our sakes became poor" (cf. 2 Corinthians 8:14; 1 John 3:17). Calvin adds that such "duties of charity... testify the piety of men... so as to detect the hypocrisy of those who boast with the mouth what is far distant from the heart" (cf. James 2:15). Cyril of Jerusalem notes John "was the first to practise what he taught." The witnesses thus see the first fruit of repentance defined not in feeling but in open-handedness: the proof that a man has turned to God is that he shares his bread and his coat with the needy at his side.
12:
"Then came also publicans to be baptized, and said unto him, Master, what shall we do?" The witnesses dwell on the surprising penitents and their inquiry.

That tax-gatherers came at all shows the power of John's word. Bede: "what great virtue there was in the discourse of the Baptist is manifested by this, that the Publicans, nay even the soldiers, he compelled to seek counsel of him concerning their salvation." Chrysostom marvels: "great is the force of virtue that makes the rich seek the way of salvation from the poor, from him that has nothing." Ellicott notes the other Gospels do not mention this class here, though their presence "is implied in Matthew 21:32."

The publicans were a despised and oppressive class. Robertson explains the word is "made up from telos, tax, and ōneomai, to buy" — "the renter or collector of taxes was not popular anywhere, but least of all when a Jew collected taxes for the Romans and did it by terrible graft and extortions." Barnes adds "there is reason to think that the publicans... were peculiarly oppressive and hard in their dealings."

Their question repeats the people's. "Master, what shall we do?" — the same earnest inquiry, now from those whose calling laid them especially open to sin. Calvin draws the principle: repentance is not "exhorted, in general terms" only, but "the duties peculiar to their calling are demanded; for besides the general rule of the law, each person ought to consider what is required by the nature of the employment to which he has been called." The witnesses thus find in the coming of the publicans both a token of John's penetrating ministry — reaching even the outcasts of society — and a pattern of true repentance: it does not stop at general sorrow but asks how the change must show itself in the particular duties and temptations of one's own occupation. Clarke notes the office, "though detested by the Jews at large," was not condemned by John; "it is only the abuse of it that he speaks against," for "if taxes be necessary for the support of a state, there must be collectors of them."
13:
"And he said unto them, Exact no more than that which is appointed you." The witnesses dwell on the reform required of the publicans and the lawfulness of their office.

John does not bid them quit their work, but do it honestly. Gill: he answers "not by advising them to quit their employments, as if it was a thing unlawful to impose pay, and collect taxes, but by directing them to perform their office aright." Bede: "he commands them therefore that they exact no more than what was appointed to them," that "by first keeping themselves from desiring other men's goods, they might at length come to share their own with their neighbours."

The temptation lay in the system. Ellicott notes that "under the farming system of taxation adopted by the Roman empire, this was the besetting temptation of all collectors... and it led naturally to the evil repute which attached... to the name of publican." Lightfoot adds that "when the Rabbins saw that the publicans exacted too much, they rejected them, as not... fit to give their testimony," and praises one collector who "gently and honestly executed that trust."

The office itself is honourable. Barnes draws the principle: "John does not condemn the office, or say that the employment should be forsaken. Though it was hated by the people... yet the office itself was not dishonourable. If there is a government, it must be supported; and of course there must be men whose duty it is to collect taxes" — and "the people should pay cheerfully the just apportionment of their rulers" (cf. Romans 13:6). The witnesses thus see John's answer as a model of practical repentance: it neither flatters the publican's sin nor demands he abandon a lawful calling, but requires that he purge it of the extortion that had made it infamous — collecting only what is due, and so turning an instrument of oppression into honest service. Lightfoot illustrates from the Rabbis, who esteemed an honest collector so rare that one who "gently and honestly executed that trust" for thirteen years was singled out for praise; John's simple rule, faithfully kept, would have made every publican such a man.
14:
"And the soldiers likewise demanded of him, saying, And what shall we do? And he said unto them, Do violence to no man, neither accuse any falsely; and be content with your wages." The witnesses dwell on the soldiers, their besetting sins, and the lawfulness of their calling.

The soldiers were likely Herod's troops. Gill thinks them "Jews... in the pay of the Romans," belonging to Antipas or Philip; Ellicott suggests "the troops of Antipas on their way down the valley of the Jordan to attack Aretas." John meets them, too, with duties fitted to their work. "Do violence to no man" (diaseisēte), Robertson explains, means "thoroughly to terrify, to extort money or property by intimidating" — blackmail, the Latin concutere (Clarke, Ellicott), "a constant temptation to soldiers." "Neither accuse any falsely" (sykophantēsēte) draws on the Athenian "fig-shower" or informer (Robertson, Ellicott, Barnes), the malignant calumniator who extorted by threat of accusation.

"Be content with your wages" strikes at a soldier's discontent. The word (opsōnia), Robertson notes, meant first "rations," then "a soldier's pay or allowance" (cf. 1 Corinthians 9:7; Romans 6:23); "discontent with wages was a complaint of mercenary soldiers."

That John reforms rather than forbids the calling the witnesses stress. Augustine (on v. 10) answers why John "did not say, Put away your arms, abandon warfare": "he knew that soldiers, when they use their arms, are not homicides, but the ministers of the law... the defenders of the public safety"; the evils to blame in war are "the desire of injury, the cruelty of revenge... the lust of power," not the office itself. Barnes agrees: "it is lawful to defend one's self, one's family, or one's country, and hence it is lawful to be a soldier"; "man everywhere... should be a Christian, and then he will do honour to his profession." Calvin too: John "does not order the soldiers to throw away their arms... but he forbids them to pillage." The witnesses thus find John's threefold charge a charter for the Christian soldier: not to extort, not to inform falsely, not to grumble for more — proving repentance, as the publicans must, within an honest calling rather than by deserting it.
15:
"And as the people were in expectation, and all men mused in their hearts of John, whether he were the Christ, or not;" The witnesses dwell on the people's wondering, the danger of misplaced honour, and John's discernment.

The deference paid to John was great, and threatened to overreach. Origen: "it was meet that more deference should be paid to John than to other men, for he lived such as no other man... only they kept not within due bounds." He warns that "love is dangerous when it is uncontrolled," for one "ought... not to love more than the object deserves." Ambrose marks the absurdity of their expectation: "what could be more absurd than that he who was fancied to be in another should not be believed in his own person?" — they would make John the Christ, yet not believe Christ truly come of a virgin.

John neither sought nor accepted the honour. The Greek Exposition notes "John gloried not in the estimation in which all held him... but embraced the lowest humility." Bede recalls that the people not only mused but, as another Evangelist relates, "sending Priests and Levites to him asked him whether he was the Christ or not" (cf. John 1:19).

His answer sprang from spiritual insight. Ambrose notes "John saw into the secrets of the heart; but let us remember by whose grace, for it is of the gift of God to reveal things to man." And he distinguishes the two baptisms his ministry pointed to: "there was one baptism of repentance, another of grace... by water the body is washed, by the Spirit the soul is cleansed." The witnesses thus see the moment of John's greatest temptation — a whole nation ready to crown him — met by his greatest humility: discerning their hearts and his own true place, he turns their gaze at once from himself to the mightier One coming after, refusing an honour that was not his and reserving it for Christ alone (cf. John 1:20). Origen adds that "he who loves any one ought to consider the nature and causes of loving, and not to love more than the object deserves," lest "both he who loves and he who is loved will be in sin" — a caution the people forgot in their zeal for John.
16:
"John answered, saying unto them all, I indeed baptize you with water; but one mightier than I cometh, the latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to unloose: he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost and with fire:" The witnesses dwell on John's public disclaimer, his self-abasement, and the greater baptism he foretells.

John answers openly to put away all doubt. Gill notes he "addressed himself, in a very public manner, to the whole multitude... that he might not have an honour conferred on him, which did not belong to him." Of "one mightier than I" Ambrose says "he makes no comparison, for there can be none between the Son of God and man... no one is mightier but Christ." Robertson notes John "was able to take his own measure in comparison with the Messiah and was loyal to him."

The image of the shoes marks his lowliness. Augustine reconciles the Evangelists — to "bear" the shoes (Matthew) and to "loose the latchet" (Luke) "have still kept the same sense... by the mention of shoes" expressing "humility." Gregory finds a deeper note: John "is not able to fathom the mystery of the Incarnation," for "our Lord being made flesh appeared as it were with shoes, as taking upon himself the carcase of our corruption" (cf. John 1:27).

The greater baptism is of Spirit and fire. Chrysostom notes that "having said that his own baptism was only with water, he next shows the excellence of that baptism which was brought by Christ." Robertson draws both senses together: the "fire" shows "the Messiah's coming as a judgment upon the world," yet John "had the spiritual conception also, the baptism in the Holy Spirit which will characterize the Messiah's Mission" (cf. Acts 1:5; Matthew 3:11). The witnesses thus see John drawing the sharpest line between himself and Christ: his water can only wash the body and seal repentance, but the Coming One will baptize the soul with the Holy Ghost — and, for the unrepentant, with unquenchable fire. Gregory adds a further image: the loosing of the shoe recalls the ancient custom by which a kinsman who declined to claim a betrothed bride loosed his shoe; John, refusing the bridegroom's name, owns that he cannot loose the shoe of the true Bridegroom, whose place he will not usurp.
17:
"Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and will gather the wheat into his garner; but the chaff he will burn with fire unquenchable." The witnesses dwell on the winnowing image and the judgment it foretells.

The fan is the Messiah's sifting power. Bede (on v. 15) explains that "without the wind the wheat and chaff cannot be separated, therefore he has the fan in his hand, which shows some to be chaff, some wheat" — the testing that "brings to light the virtue which was hid in you," or exposes the unbelieving as chaff. Robertson (on v. 16) notes John "saw the Messiah's coming as a judgment upon the world like fire and the fan of the thrashing-floor."

The harvest is divided. The threshing-floor is "throughly" purged — the better manuscripts, Ellicott notes, read "throughly to purge, and to gather." The wheat is gathered "into his garner" (cf. Micah 4:12; Matthew 13:30), but "the chaff he will burn." The imagery is drawn straight from the prophets and psalms — the fan of Jeremiah 15:7, the chaff of Psalm 1:4 (Barnes).

The fire is "unquenchable." The witnesses take the word in its full and dreadful sense (cf. Mark 9:48): not a fire soon spent, but the everlasting punishment of the impenitent. The two ends — the garner and the unquenchable fire — set before John's hearers the whole issue of the Messiah's coming. He comes not only to baptize with the Spirit but to judge, separating, by the wind of his sifting, the true from the false among those who profess his name. The witnesses thus see in the homely picture of the threshing-floor a solemn warning: every soul will prove, in the end, either wheat for the garner or chaff for the fire, and the Christ whom John heralds is the One in whose hand the fan already lies. The call to repentance (v. 8) and the threat of the axe (v. 9) here reach their climax in the figure of the harvest, where mercy and judgment meet. Gill notes the floor is "his" floor — the visible church, the professing people — so that the sifting falls first among those who bear the name, dividing the true grain from the worthless husk that has grown up alongside it.
18:
"And many other things in his exhortation preached he unto the people." The witnesses dwell on the breadth of John's preaching and its gospel character.

Luke gives only a sample. Origen notes that "as in the Gospel according to St. John it is related of Christ that he spoke many other things, so also... we must understand Luke to say the same of John the Baptist, since certain things are announced by John too great to be entrusted to writing" (cf. John 21:25). Robertson: "Luke has given a bare sample of the wonderful messages of the Baptist. Few as his words preserved are, they give a definite and powerful conception of his preaching."

His preaching was gospel. Theophylact notes "his exhortation was the telling of good things, and therefore is fitly called the Gospel" — the verb Luke uses is the very word for preaching good news. Gill fills out the content: "relating to the person and office of the Messiah... the Gospel dispensation, and to faith in him; for he pointed him out to the people, and exhorted them to believe in him."

Ellicott notes the breadth is distinctively Luke's: "St. Luke's is the only record which lays stress on the wider range of the Baptist's teaching." The particular replies to the crowds, the publicans, and the soldiers (vv. 10–14) were, as it were, "the text and burden of it all," but behind them lay a fuller ministry of exhortation and good news. The witnesses thus see John as more than a stern herald of wrath: his preaching, though it began with the axe and the fan, was at heart an evangel — a pointing forward to the Christ and a summons to believe in him. What the Evangelist preserves is a window onto a far richer ministry, by which a careless people were both warned of judgment and pointed to the Lamb of God. Ellicott observes that the sources which gave Luke the replies to the crowds, publicans, and soldiers "probably brought to his knowledge much of the same character," so that the recorded sayings are representative samples of a long and varied preaching, not its whole sum.
19:
"But Herod the tetrarch, being reproved by him for Herodias his brother Philip's wife, and for all the evils which Herod had done," The witnesses dwell on the reproof, its cause, and the courage it required.

This Herod is distinguished from the king of the nativity. Eusebius notes "he is called the tetrarch, to distinguish him from the other Herod, in whose reign Christ was born, and who was king, but this Herod was tetrarch." The cause of the reproof was his marriage: "he had sacrilegiously married his brother Philip's wife, though she had offspring by his brother" — a thing lawful only "whose brothers died without issue" (cf. Matthew 14:3; Leviticus 18:16). Robertson notes "Josephus shows how repulsive this marriage was to Jewish feeling," and that "reproved" (elenchō) means to "convict" or "expose."

John reproved not this sin only but "all the evils" Herod had done — a full and fearless rebuke of the tetrarch's whole course.

Such courage Calvin holds up as a pattern. "This history shows clearly what sort of reward awaits the faithful and honest ministers of the truth, particularly when they reprove vices: for scarcely one in a hundred bears reproof." In John "we behold an illustrious example of that moral courage which all pious teachers ought to possess, not to hesitate to incur the wrath of the great and powerful... for he with whom there is acceptance of persons does not honestly serve God." Origen marks the same: John "feared not Herod, nor dreaded death." The witnesses thus see John faithful to the last in his prophetic office — sparing not even a prince, naming his sin to his face, and so fulfilling the calling of every true minister: to declare God's word without flattery, whatever the cost. It was this fidelity, not any fault of his own, that brought him to the prison and the sword. Calvin warns that "if pride of this sort displays itself in some of the common people, we have no reason to wonder that cruelty to reprovers assumes a more hideous form in tyrants," who "brook nothing worse than to be classed with other men"; the greater the power, the fiercer the resentment of rebuke.
20:
"Added yet this above all, that he shut up John in prison." The witnesses dwell on the climax of Herod's sin and Luke's placing of the event.

The imprisonment crowned Herod's crimes. Gill calls it the sin "added to all other sins, and which was of a more flagitious nature." Calvin reads "above all" as marking how far his malice had gone: "Herod's malice is become desperate, and has reached its utmost height, when the sinner is enraged by remedies, and not only refuses correction, but takes vengeance on his adviser, as if he had been his enemy." Barnes is careful that "above all" does not mean "this was the worst of his acts, but that this was one of his deeds, of like character as the others."

Luke records it here out of chronological order. Wesley explains: "this circumstance, though it happened after, is here mentioned before our Lord's baptism, that his history (that of John being concluded) may then follow without any interruption." Bede agrees that John "was not imprisoned in those days" but is mentioned now; the Gloss adds that Luke, "before he relates any of the acts of Jesus, says that John was taken by Herod, to show that he alone was in an especial manner going to describe those of our Lord's acts which were performed since" John's removal.

The witnesses thus see Luke rounding off the Baptist's story before turning to Christ. The forerunner's course ends as it had run — in fearless fidelity met by the world's hatred. Herod, who had "heard him gladly" (as another Evangelist tells), at last silenced the voice that troubled his conscience, shutting up in prison the greatest born of women. The placing of the notice is deliberate and theological: John's witness now complete, the stage is cleared for the One he heralded. The lamp is hidden away, that the Light itself may shine. The next verses turn from the herald to the Lord, from the voice to the Word, as Jesus comes to be baptized and to begin the ministry for which John had prepared the way.
21:
"Now when all the people were baptized, it came to pass, that Jesus also being baptized, and praying, the heaven was opened," The witnesses dwell on why the sinless One was baptized, and on the opened heaven.

Christ was baptized not to be cleansed but to cleanse. Ambrose: "our Lord was baptized not that he might be cleansed by the waters but to cleanse them, that being purified by the flesh of Christ who knew no sin, they might possess the power of baptism." His own reason he gave: "thus it becometh us to fulfil all righteousness" (cf. Matthew 3:15); "let none then avoid the laver of grace, since Christ avoided not the laver of repentance." Gregory Nazianzen adds he came "to bury the old Adam in water."

Chrysostom distinguishes the baptisms. The Jewish "removed the pollutions of the flesh," John's "taught them to turn from sin to virtue" but "conveyed not the Holy Spirit," and Christian baptism "parts us from sin... and gives us largely the outpouring of the Spirit." Christ "needed not the pardon of sins, nor was that flesh destitute of the Holy Spirit"; he was baptized "that... you might know that he was not baptized because he needed the gift."

Luke alone adds that he was "praying." Chrysostom draws the lesson: "how fitting to one who has received baptism is constant prayer." And "the heaven was opened" — Chrysostom: "the higher and the lower sheepfold being brought into one... the heavens opened, and man was incorporated a fellow citizen with the Angels." Bede: "therein is shown the virtue of baptism, that when a man comes forth from it the gates of the heavenly kingdom are opened to him." The witnesses thus see in Christ's baptism the consecration of ours: the sinless Son stoops to the water not for his own need but for our sake — to fulfil all righteousness, to hallow the laver, and to fling open the heaven that sin had shut. Bede adds that the open heaven shows the believer that, though all sins are forgiven in baptism, "not as yet is the weakness of this fleshly substance made strong" — having crossed the Red Sea, we still meet foes in the wilderness of this life, to be subdued by Christ's grace until we reach our own country.
22:
"And the Holy Ghost descended in a bodily shape like a dove upon him, and a voice came from heaven, which said, Thou art my beloved Son; in thee I am well pleased." The witnesses dwell on the dove, the Spirit's descent, and the Father's voice.

The Spirit appeared in visible form. "In a bodily shape," Ellicott notes, is "peculiar to St. Luke," and Robertson takes it that "the Baptist saw the vision that looked like a dove" (cf. John 1:33). The dove's symbolism the fathers expound: Ambrose, that "the grace of baptism requires innocence, that we should be innocent as doves," and "peace, which under the emblem of an olive branch the dove once brought" to the ark; Chrysostom, that it shows "the meekness of the Lord," whereas at Pentecost the Spirit came "like fire, to signify punishment" (cf. Acts 2:3).

The descent was for us. Chrysostom: "the Holy Spirit descended upon Christ as upon the Founder of our race, that he might be in Christ first of all who received him not for himself, but rather for us" — "as God he sent him from above, and as man received him below." Augustine adds it "prefigured his body, i.e. the Church, in which the baptized especially receive the Holy Spirit."

The voice reveals the Son. Robertson notes "the Trinity here manifest themselves at the baptism of Jesus," and "the deity of Christ here appears in plain form." Athanasius presses "beloved Son" against Arius: Christ is not Son "as they are called who obtain the name through grace," but "really and by nature the Son... the Only begotten," as "Isaac is the son of Abraham" (cf. Matthew 3:17). The witnesses thus see at the Jordan the threefold God revealed — the Son in the water, the Spirit as a dove, the Father in the voice — opening Christ's Messianic ministry with the Father's blessing and the Spirit's power, and declaring to the world the eternal, beloved Sonship of the One there baptized. Cyril of Jerusalem notes the descent was "not that Jesus might see Him first, for He knew Him even before He came in a bodily shape, but that John, who was baptizing Him, might behold Him" — the sign given for the forerunner's sake, that he might point the Lamb out to Israel (John 1:33).
23:
"And Jesus himself began to be about thirty years of age, being (as was supposed) the son of Joseph, which was the son of Heli," The witnesses dwell on the age, the upward genealogy, and the famous question of the two lines.

Luke's genealogy ascends. Origen notes he carries it "not... from the higher to the lower, but beginning with Christ, he carries it up to God himself." The age of thirty the fathers read as instructive: Gregory Nazianzen draws lessons "of purifying ourselves beforehand... of embracing humility, and of not beginning to preach until the maturity of our spiritual and natural life"; Chrysostom, that Christ "waited accomplishing the whole law until that age which takes in every sin." Bede sees in "thrice ten years" a hint of "faith in the Trinity, and obedience to the Decalogue."

"As was supposed the son of Joseph" the witnesses qualify. Ambrose: "rightly as was supposed, since in reality he was not, but was supposed to be so, because Mary, who was espoused to Joseph, was his mother." Scripture "always seeks the origin of the husband," and in Joseph's line "we also find that of Mary," both being of David's house (cf. Luke 1:27).

The divergence from Matthew the witnesses address at length. Matthew traces the line through Solomon, Luke through Nathan (cf. 2 Samuel 5:14); Matthew calls Joseph son of Jacob (Matthew 1:16), Luke son of Heli. Augustine resolves it by levirate succession — "by the same reason by which the Saviour is called the son of Joseph, he is also the son of Heli." Eusebius offers two readings: that Matthew "gives the real truth," while Luke "repeats the common opinion of many" (hence "as was supposed"); and, more deeply, that Matthew, writing of the descent into the flesh, descends, while Luke, "hastening forward to the regeneration which takes place in baptism, rises up from the lowest to the highest," up to God. The witnesses thus see the genealogy fitly placed after the baptism: as Christ is declared the Son of God by the voice, Luke traces his human line upward, past Joseph and David, to Adam and God — the new Adam joined to the old race he came to redeem.
24:
"Which was the son of Matthat, which was the son of Levi, which was the son of Melchi, which was the son of Janna, which was the son of Joseph," The witnesses dwell on these obscure post-exilic links and the transmission of the genealogy.

This portion of the line lies in the silent centuries between the Testaments, and the names are otherwise unknown to Scripture. Gill notes a textual question at the head of it: Matthat and Levi, he reports, "are omitted in the ancient exemplars," and Grotius "thinks they ought to be left out," citing "Irenaeus, Africanus, Eusebius, Nazianzen, Jerome, and Augustine"; yet, Gill adds, "not only the Vulgate Latin, but all the Oriental versions, retain them." The genealogy thus shows, even in its smallest links, the care with which the line was handed down and the variations that crept into its copying.

Barnes simply refers the reader to the parallel discussion of the genealogy in Matthew (cf. Matthew 1:1), where the differences between the two records are weighed. Here Luke's ascending list passes through generations of whom nothing else is told — men who held no throne and left no story, yet through whom the promised Seed was brought forward toward his birth.

The witnesses find in such obscure names a quiet testimony. Eusebius had observed (on v. 23) that Luke, "hastening forward to the regeneration which takes place in baptism," rises "from the lowest to the highest," tracing the line upward to God. These hidden generations are part of that ascent: the long, unbroken chain by which God kept his word to David and to Abraham through ages when no prophet spoke and no king reigned. That the Spirit should preserve and the Evangelist record names the world had wholly forgotten shows, the witnesses observe, that no link in the purpose of God is lost — and that the humanity Christ took was a real descent through real, if unremembered, men. Eusebius had taught (on v. 23) that Luke, tracing the line of the regenerate, "relates those who have led a virtuous life in the sight of God"; even these forgotten names stand in that honoured succession by which the promise was carried forward.
25:
"Which was the son of Mattathias, which was the son of Amos, which was the son of Naum, which was the son of Esli, which was the son of Nagge," The witnesses dwell on these unrecorded generations and the hidden faithfulness of God.

Like the verse before, this segment names men found nowhere else in the sacred record. Gill, tracing the line, can add only fragments from later Jewish reckonings — that Mattathias was "surnamed Siloah... who governed ten years" — fragments that show how little was remembered of these intervening fathers. They belong to the long stretch after the return from exile, when the prophetic voice had fallen silent and the house of David lived on in obscurity.

The genealogy's value here lies precisely in its obscurity. Where Matthew's descending line dwells on kings, Luke's ascending line passes through unknown men, and the witnesses, following Eusebius (on v. 23), see in this Luke's design: he "keeps out of sight those sinners of whom Matthew makes mention" and traces the line of a quieter, humbler descent. These were not rulers but links — generation after generation faithfully begetting the next, that the promise might be kept.

The witnesses thus find in the bare list a hidden sermon on the providence of God. Through centuries of silence, with no throne to mark the line and no prophet to name its heirs, God preserved the seed of David unbroken until "the fulness of the time was come" (cf. Galatians 4:4). That these names survive at all, when so much else perished, testifies that the Lord forgot neither his covenant nor the obscure men through whom he kept it. The genealogy assures the reader that the long delay between promise and fulfilment was no failure of God's word, but its patient working through the ordinary succession of generations toward the appointed hour of the Saviour's coming. Origen had observed (on v. 23) that the line is carried "up to God himself"; each of these silent generations is a step in that long ascent, a reminder that God numbers and keeps even those whom history forgets.
26:
"Which was the son of Maath, which was the son of Mattathias, which was the son of Semei, which was the son of Joseph, which was the son of Juda," The witnesses dwell on the continuing obscure descent and the steadiness of the line.

Again the names are unknown to the rest of Scripture, and Gill can offer only the scantiest gloss — that Maath was "surnamed Aser, who governed nine years." The repetition of common Jewish names through this part of the list — a Mattathias here as in the verse before, a Joseph, a Juda — marks generations distinguished by nothing but their place in the line of promise.

The very monotony of the record carries its lesson. The genealogy is not adorned with deeds or titles; it simply affirms, link by link, that the descent held. Ambrose had noted (on v. 23) that "Scripture always seeks the origin of the husband," tracing the legal line through which inheritance and promise passed; and through these unremarkable men that line ran on unbroken from David toward Joseph and Mary.

The witnesses find here the same comfort the earlier links afford: that God's purposes advance as much through hidden, ordinary faithfulness as through the great and named. Origen had observed (on v. 23) that Luke "carries up" the line "to God himself"; these obscure fathers are rungs in that ascent. Each, in his own generation, received the line from those before and handed it to those after — and in so doing served, unknowing, the coming of the Christ. The list thus dignifies the obscure: men whose names the world would never have kept are kept by the Spirit, because through them the Saviour came. It reminds the reader that the great work of redemption was prepared not only in famous reigns but in the quiet succession of forgotten households faithful to their God across the generations. Eusebius noted (on v. 23) that to him who is "born again in God" Luke "ascribes parents who are according to God"; the obscure fathers of this list are honoured not for fame but for their place in the line of the coming Redeemer.
27:
"Which was the son of Joanna, which was the son of Rhesa, which was the son of Zorobabel, which was the son of Salathiel, which was the son of Neri," The witnesses dwell on the link to the return from exile and the puzzles of the names.

Here the line touches a known moment of history: Zerubbabel, who led the return from Babylon (cf. Ezra 5:2; Haggai 1:1). Lightfoot notes that Pedaiah, named as Zerubbabel's father in 1 Chronicles 3:19, is here passed over, as he is in Ezra and Haggai. He offers a striking explanation of the differing names: "Zorobabel and his sons were carried out of Babylon into Judea, and possibly they might change their names when they changed the place of their dwelling." It "was not very safe" to bear in Babylon the name Zerubbabel, which means "the winnowing of Babel"; "but he might securely resume the name in Judea, when Cyrus and Darius had now fanned and sifted Babylon."

The names of the sons, Lightfoot adds, suited Judea but not exile: Rhesa, "a prince," and Abiud, "the glory of my father," were titles a captive could not safely claim, but a returned exile might. So the genealogy bears, in its very names, the memory of bondage and restoration.

The link to Salathiel and Neri raises the old question of the two genealogies (cf. Matthew 1:12), where Matthew names Jechonias as Salathiel's father. Lightfoot holds "Neri was indeed the father of Salathiel," the lines converging at this point. The witnesses thus see the genealogy passing through the great hinge of the exile: the house of David, brought low and carried captive, yet preserved and brought home, the promise surviving Babylon as the wheat survives the winnowing. Through the very man whose name spoke of Babel's sifting, the line of the Messiah was carried over the chasm of the captivity toward its fulfilment. Gill adds that Zerubbabel and his sons, carried out of Babylon into Judea, may well have borne different names in the two lands, which helps account for the variation between Luke's list and the records of Ezra and the Chronicles.
28:
"Which was the son of Melchi, which was the son of Addi, which was the son of Cosam, which was the son of Elmodam, which was the son of Er," The witnesses dwell on the pre-exilic descent and Luke's line through Nathan.

Gill marks the historical place of this segment: these, "with the following... all lived before the captivity, and were of the house of David, in the line of Nathan." Here is the distinctive feature of Luke's genealogy. Where Matthew descends through Solomon and the royal kings of Judah, Luke ascends through Nathan, another son of David — a line that never sat on the throne but carried the blood of David through humbler channels.

The witnesses, following the fathers' discussion (on v. 23), find this divergence significant. Eusebius had noted that "many affirmed that Christ would come through Solomon and the other kings," while others "shunned this opinion because of the many crimes related of their kings," and because of Jeremiah's word that of Jechoniah's seed "a man should not... sit on the throne of David." Luke's line through Nathan thus traces a descent untainted by the curse pronounced on the royal house, bringing the Messiah from David by a path apart from the failed kings.

These pre-captivity names, like the post-exilic ones, are otherwise unknown — generations of the Nathan branch preserved only here. The witnesses see in them the same faithful providence: God kept not one line only but, as it were, a reserve, that when the royal line was cut off by judgment, the promise to David might still stand. Through Nathan's obscure descendants the blood of the king flowed on, hidden from history but kept by God, until it met, in Joseph and Mary, the line of promise and brought forth the Christ. The genealogy thus quietly affirms that the failure of the kings did not defeat the covenant: God had another way prepared. Eusebius had explained (on v. 23) that some "shunned" the line of the royal kings "because of the many crimes related of them"; Luke's descent through Nathan traces the blood of David by a purer and humbler channel toward the Christ.
30:
"Which was the son of Simeon, which was the son of Juda, which was the son of Joseph, which was the son of Jonan, which was the son of Eliakim," The witnesses dwell on these unremembered links and the reality of Christ's human descent.

This segment, deep in the Nathan branch before the exile, names men of whom Scripture tells nothing further. The names themselves are ancient and honoured — Simeon, Judah, Joseph — borne here by descendants of David far removed from the throne, links in a chain whose only recorded importance is that it led to Christ.

The witnesses find the very anonymity instructive. The genealogy does not embellish; it simply records that the line continued through real men in real generations. This is the ground of a great truth: that the Son of God took a true and traceable human nature. He did not appear as a phantom or descend from heaven full-grown, but was born into a genuine human family with a genuine human ancestry, "made like unto his brethren" (cf. Hebrews 2:17). The long list of forgotten fathers is itself a witness to the incarnation.

Eusebius had drawn out (on v. 23) the deeper sense of Luke's ascending order: rising "from the lowest to the highest," it reaches at last to God, tracing the new Adam back through the old race he came to redeem. These obscure generations are part of that ascent — the human roots that bind the Saviour to the whole family of man. The witnesses thus see in the bare names a confession of faith: the Word was made flesh, and his flesh had a history, reaching back through Simeon and Judah and Joseph, through David and Abraham, to Adam himself. That history, however little of it the world remembered, God thought fit to preserve, that we might know the Redeemer as truly bone of our bone, sprung from a line as real as our own. Ambrose had noted (on v. 23) that Scripture "always seeks the origin of the husband"; through these unremembered fathers of Joseph's line the legal descent of the Saviour was faithfully carried, that he might be reckoned, as the law required, a true son of David's house.
31:
"Which was the son of Melea, which was the son of Menan, which was the son of Mattatha, which was the son of Nathan, which was the son of David," The witnesses dwell on the great turning-point of the line at David through Nathan.

Here the ascending genealogy reaches David, the head of the royal house and the great recipient of the messianic promise. Barnes notes that the line runs through "Nathan" (cf. 2 Samuel 5:14; Zechariah 12:12) — not the prophet Nathan, but a son born to David in Jerusalem, distinct from Solomon. This is the node on which the whole distinctive character of Luke's genealogy turns: from David downward, Matthew and Luke diverge, the one through Solomon, the other through Nathan.

The reaching of David is the genealogy's great confirmation. To him the promise had been made that of his seed should come an everlasting King (cf. Luke 1:32, "the Lord God shall give unto him the throne of his father David"). All the obscure generations before this verse have been climbing toward, and now descend from, this name; David is the point where the hidden line joins the open promise of Scripture.

The witnesses, recalling the fathers' discussion (on v. 23), see the choice of Nathan's line as deliberate. The Messiah is brought from David, as prophecy required, yet by a branch apart from the kings on whom judgment had fallen. Zechariah himself had named "the family of the house of Nathan" among the mourning tribes of the last days (Zech 12:12), giving the branch a place in prophecy. The witnesses thus find in this verse the meeting of the human and the promised: the long ascent of unknown fathers arrives at David, and in David at the covenant of the kingdom, assuring the reader that the child traced through all these generations is indeed the Son of David, the heir of the everlasting throne. Gill and the fathers (on v. 23) agree that from David the two Gospels part — Matthew descending by Solomon and the kings, Luke ascending by Nathan — so that this name marks the very hinge on which the harmony of the genealogies turns.
32:
"Which was the son of Jesse, which was the son of Obed, which was the son of Booz, which was the son of Salmon, which was the son of Naasson," The witnesses dwell on this well-known stretch and its agreement with the older Scriptures.

From David back, the line enters familiar ground. Gill notes that "the order of the persons from Jesse to Abraham" — Obed, Boaz, Salmon, Nahshon, and the rest — "perfectly agrees with the genealogy of Matthew, and the accounts of the Old Testament." Here Luke and Matthew run together, for both follow the same ancient record (cf. Ruth 4:18–22; Matthew 1:5).

These are names rich in story. Jesse the Bethlehemite, father of David; Obed, son of Boaz and Ruth the Moabitess; Boaz the kinsman-redeemer; Salmon, husband of Rahab — the line carries within it the memory of Ruth and of Rahab, Gentile women grafted into the ancestry of the Christ. The genealogy here is no bare list but the close of the book of Ruth, where this very pedigree is set down to show how the house of David sprang from the union of Boaz and his redeemed bride.

The witnesses find in this convergence a confirmation of both Gospels. Where Luke's earlier links ran through obscure and unrecorded men, here his line is checked against Matthew and against the Old Testament, and found to agree exactly. The reader is thus assured that the ascending genealogy, however unfamiliar in its later reaches, rests at its base on the firm ground of established Scripture. And the presence in it of Ruth's household, with its Gentile strand, quietly foreshadows the gospel Luke loves to proclaim: that the salvation springing from this line is for all flesh, Gentile as well as Jew, gathered like Ruth into the family of God's people through the kinsman-redeemer who would come of this very stock. The witnesses note that here, where Luke and the Old Testament agree exactly, the genealogy is most easily verified; the reader who doubts the obscurer links may rest on this firm and well-attested stretch from Jesse back to the patriarchs.
34:
"Which was the son of Jacob, which was the son of Isaac, which was the son of Abraham, which was the son of Thara, which was the son of Nachor," The witnesses dwell on the patriarchs and the covenant of promise.

The line now reaches the great fathers of the faith. Barnes refers the names to their origin in Genesis 11:24–26: Terah the father of Abraham, and back to Nahor and the line of Shem. Above all it reaches Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — the patriarchs to whom the promises were made.

This is the point at which Matthew's genealogy began. Matthew, writing for the Jews, opens with "Abraham" and descends to Christ; Luke, having ascended from Christ, passes through Abraham and presses on beyond him, up to Adam and to God. The witnesses, following Eusebius (on v. 23), see the difference as deliberate: Matthew shows Christ the son of Abraham, heir of the covenant of Israel; Luke shows him the son of Adam, kinsman of the whole human race.

The reaching of Abraham is the genealogy's covenantal centre. To him it was said, "in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed" (cf. Genesis 12:3; Galatians 3:16, "to thy seed, which is Christ"). All the line between Abraham and Jesus is the unfolding of that single promise; the child traced through these generations is the Seed in whom the nations are blessed. The witnesses thus find in this verse the meeting of genealogy and gospel: the Saviour is shown to be the true son of Abraham, in whom the ancient covenant is at last fulfilled, and the blessing promised to one family opened, through him, to all the families of the earth. Beyond Abraham the line will climb to Adam, binding the covenant of grace to the whole of fallen humanity. Eusebius had drawn the contrast (on v. 23) that Matthew, writing of the descent into the flesh, begins with Abraham and descends, while Luke ascends past Abraham toward God; the same patriarch thus stands as the meeting-point and the parting-point of the two records.
35:
"Which was the son of Saruch, which was the son of Ragau, which was the son of Phalec, which was the son of Heber, which was the son of Sala," The witnesses dwell on the post-flood patriarchs and the line of Shem.

Beyond Abraham the genealogy enters the world after the flood, among the descendants of Shem listed in Genesis 11:16–18: Serug, Reu, Peleg, Eber, Salah. Gill, tracing the names, notes only that "Saruch" is "the same with Serug," matching the Hebrew record beneath the Greek forms Luke uses.

Two of these names carry their own memory. Eber, from whom many derive the name "Hebrew," stands as a kind of father of the chosen stock. And Peleg, the witnesses recall, was so named "for in his days was the earth divided" (cf. Genesis 10:25) — the dispersion of the nations at Babel. The genealogy thus passes through the very generation in which mankind was scattered into peoples and tongues.

The witnesses find in this a fitting note for Luke's universal Gospel. The line of the Saviour runs back not only through Abraham's chosen family but through the post-flood fathers of all nations, to the very point where the peoples divided. Origen had observed (on v. 23) that Luke carries the line "up to God himself"; this stretch carries it through the common ancestors of the whole earth. The Christ who springs from this line is therefore kinsman not of Israel only but of every nation descended from Noah's sons. The dividing of the earth in Peleg's day, the scattering at Babel, finds its answer in the One whose genealogy gathers all these branching peoples back to a single root — and who would send his gospel to reunite, in himself, the nations that were there divided. The bare names thus trace the breadth of his kinship with mankind. Origen had said (on v. 23) that Luke carries the line "up to God himself"; passing here through the fathers of all the post-flood nations, the genealogy shows the Saviour's kinship reaching beyond Israel to the whole family of man.
36:
"Which was the son of Cainan, which was the son of Arphaxad, which was the son of Sem, which was the son of Noe, which was the son of Lamech," The witnesses dwell on the famous textual crux of this Cainan and on the line through Noah.

This verse carries one of the best-known difficulties in the genealogy: the second "Cainan," named here as son of Arphaxad. Gill states the problem forcefully: "this Cainan is not mentioned by Moses... nor in any Hebrew copy of the Old Testament, nor in the Samaritan version, nor in the Targum; nor is he mentioned by Josephus" (cf. Genesis 11:12, where Arphaxad is father of Salah directly). Gill judges it "owing to some early negligent transcriber," and "ought not to stand neither in the text, nor in any version."

Lightfoot agrees Luke "hath herein followed the Greek version," the Septuagint, which inserts Cainan between Arphaxad and Salah, though the Hebrew omits him; Clarke notes the name "is not found in any other Scripture genealogy," and suggests it may be a surname of Salah, or simply a copyist's intrusion. The witnesses thus record a genuine debate over whether the name is original to Luke or a later gloss carried in from the Greek.

Whatever the resolution, the line itself is sure: it runs through Shem, son of Noah, and so through the flood (cf. Genesis 10:21). Here the genealogy reaches the second father of the human race, the man through whom mankind was preserved when the world perished in the waters. The witnesses see the descent passing through this great deliverance: as the race was saved in Noah from the flood, so from Noah's line would come the Saviour of the world. The textual question over Cainan, they note, touches only a single link and leaves untouched the great fact — that the Christ is traced through Noah and Shem to the very beginnings of the renewed world. Lightfoot observes that the difficulty arises wholly from Luke's use of the Greek version, where the name stands, against the Hebrew, where it does not; the question is one of textual transmission, not of the genuineness of the line itself, which runs sure through Noah to the renewed world.
37:
"Which was the son of Mathusala, which was the son of Enoch, which was the son of Jared, which was the son of Maleleel, which was the son of Cainan," The witnesses dwell on the antediluvian patriarchs and the shining example of Enoch.

The line now enters the world before the flood, among the long-lived fathers of Genesis 5: Methuselah, Enoch, Jared, Mahalaleel, and the first Cainan, son of Enos. Gill's note here is the barest of all — "which was the son" — for of most of these Scripture records only their names and their great ages. Methuselah is remembered as the longest-lived of men, his years reaching almost to the very year of the flood.

One name shines out from the list. Enoch, the witnesses recall, "walked with God: and he was not; for God took him" (cf. Genesis 5:24) — translated that he should not see death, a sign in the old world of the life beyond it. He is named in the New Testament as a prophet of judgment, who foretold the Lord's coming "with ten thousands of his saints" (cf. Jude 14). That the Saviour's line should run through Enoch joins the genealogy to one of the brightest examples of faith before the flood.

The witnesses find in these antediluvian fathers the genealogy's reach back to the dawn of human history. The line of the Christ is no recent thing, sprung up with Israel; it runs back through Noah and Enoch and Seth to the very first generations of mankind. Origen had observed (on v. 23) that Luke carries the line "up to God himself," and here it nears its source. The patriarchs of the old world, who lived and begat and died in the long ages before the deluge, were links in the chain that bound the coming Redeemer to the whole human story — and Enoch, walking with God and taken to him, stands among them as an early pledge of the life and triumph over death that the Seed of this line would at last secure for all. Eusebius had observed (on v. 23) that Luke names those who "led a virtuous life in the sight of God"; among these ancient fathers Enoch stands pre-eminent, the man who walked with God and was taken, a shining witness of faith in the world before the flood.
38:
"Which was the son of Enos, which was the son of Seth, which was the son of Adam, which was the son of God." The witnesses dwell on the climax of the genealogy in Adam, the son of God.

The ascending line reaches at last its source. Through Enos and Seth it comes to Adam, and beyond Adam to God himself. Origen had marked the whole design (on v. 23): Luke, "beginning with Christ, carries it up to God." The genealogy that began with Jesus, declared at the Jordan to be the beloved Son of God, ends by naming Adam "the son of God" — binding the two together.

Adam's sonship is by creation. Ellicott explains: "the whole form of the genealogy leads us to apply these words to Adam. Humanity as such, as the result of an immediate creative act, was the offspring of God" (cf. Acts 17:28, "we are also his offspring"); and "the words of the angel" at the annunciation (Luke 1:35) imply that "it was because the human nature of our Lord originated in a like creative act, that it was entitled... to be called the Son of God." Barnes points to the same origin in Genesis 2:7 and Genesis 1:26.

The witnesses find here the deep purpose of Luke's ascending order. Where Matthew shows Christ the son of Abraham and of David, Luke shows him the son of Adam and of God — kinsman of the whole human race, not of Israel only. The placing of the genealogy after the baptism is deliberate: the Father has just owned Jesus as his Son; now the line shows him joined, through Adam, to all the children of men. Ellicott draws the parallel of the two Adams: "what was true of the second Adam was true also partly... of the first" (cf. 1 Corinthians 15:45). The witnesses thus see the genealogy close on its great theme: the Son of God become son of Adam, that the sons of Adam might become sons of God — the new Head of the race traced back to its first head and beyond, to the God who made them both.
